enrichment.short_description: Krisp is an AI app that removes background noise and echo from calls in real time and turns meetings into transcripts, summaries, and action items.
enrichment.seo_title: Krisp Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Krisp review 2026: AI noise cancellation and meeting assistant pricing, features, pros and cons, and how it compares to Otter.ai, Fireflies, and Fathom.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Krisp is an AI-powered noise cancellation and meeting assistant application founded in 2017 by Davit Baghdasaryan, formerly head of product security at Twilio, and Artavazd Minasyan, a mathematician; the company's engineering roots trace to Yerevan, Armenia, while its corporate headquarters is now in Berkeley, California. Krisp raised a $1.5 million seed round in July 2018 from investors including Sierra Ventures and Shanda, followed by a $5 million Series A in August 2020 led by Storm Ventures, Sierra Ventures, TechNexus Venture Collaborative, and Hive Ventures, which was extended by $9 million in February 2021 led by RTP Global, bringing total Series A funding to roughly $14 million and lifetime funding to about $15.5 million; the company has reportedly since scaled to tens of millions of dollars in annual revenue without raising further outside capital. Architecturally, Krisp works as a virtual audio device that sits between a computer's microphone or speakers and whatever calling application is in use, applying deep learning models to isolate a speaker's voice from background noise, echo, and even other people talking in the same room, a capability it calls Background Voice Cancellation. Because this filtering happens app-agnostically at the operating-system audio layer, Krisp works with essentially any conferencing or VoIP tool, including Zoom, Google Meet, Microsoft Teams, Slack huddles, and call center software, without requiring the other application to integrate with it directly. Beyond audio cleanup, Krisp has expanded into an AI meeting assistant that can join or listen to calls to generate live transcripts, AI-written summaries, and action items, alongside audio and video call recording and a searchable meeting history. Krisp markets its noise cancellation as processed locally on the user's device rather than streamed to the cloud, a privacy-first architecture the company positions as important for regulated industries, and its paid plans explicitly support HIPAA and GDPR compliant handling of call data. The product is priced on a freemium model: the Free plan provides 60 minutes per day of noise cancellation but excludes transcription, AI summaries, and recording; the Pro plan at $8 per month unlocks unlimited noise cancellation, unlimited AI summaries and action items, meeting history, audio and video recording, and HIPAA/GDPR-compliant handling; the Business plan at $15 per month adds CRM integrations, single sign-on, expanded storage, and team management controls; and an Enterprise tier offers custom pricing with a centralized admin dashboard, organization-wide analytics, SSO, centralized billing, and custom security policies. A seven-day free trial gives full access to all paid features, including unlimited transcription and recording, before a plan decision is required. Krisp's target audience spans individual remote workers who want quieter calls, sales and support teams that need cleaner recordings and searchable call history, and larger enterprises in healthcare, legal, or financial services that need compliant handling of voice data. In the competitive landscape, Krisp is most often compared with meeting-assistant tools such as Otter.ai, Fireflies.ai, Fathom, and Nyota; those competitors generally lead with live transcription, speaker tagging, and CRM-oriented automation (for example, Fireflies' AskFred Q&A feature and task detection for sales teams, or Otter's collaborative live-note features), whereas Krisp differentiates itself by originating from audio engineering rather than transcription, leading with real-time noise and voice cancellation as its core technology and layering meeting intelligence on top rather than the reverse. This makes Krisp a common complementary purchase alongside a dedicated transcription tool for teams that specifically struggle with noisy home offices, open-plan workspaces, or unreliable headset audio, while its growing AI meeting assistant features increasingly let it also serve as a standalone alternative to those competitors for teams that prioritize call clarity first.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Krisp is a desktop, mobile, and browser-integrated application built to solve a specific, common problem in remote work: noisy, unprofessional-sounding calls. Founded in 2017 by Davit Baghdasaryan and Artavazd Minasyan, the company built its core technology around on-device deep learning models that strip out background noise, echo, and even competing voices from a room, so that only the primary speaker comes through clearly on a call.

Because Krisp operates at the audio-driver level rather than inside any single conferencing app, it works across virtually any tool a team already uses, from Zoom and Google Meet to Microsoft Teams, Slack huddles, and call center software. Over time the company has layered an AI meeting assistant on top of this audio foundation, adding live transcription, AI-generated summaries, action items, and recording, which moves Krisp from a pure noise-cancellation utility toward a broader meeting productivity tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Krisp's signature capability is real-time AI noise cancellation, which removes background sounds like keyboard clicks, traffic, barking dogs, and construction noise from both the microphone and speaker side of a call, along with Background Voice Cancellation, which filters out other people talking nearby so only the intended speaker is heard. Its AI meeting assistant extends this into transcription, AI-written summaries, and action-item extraction, paired with audio and video recording and a searchable meeting history.

On the compliance and enterprise side, Krisp supports HIPAA and GDPR-aligned handling of call audio on paid plans, along with single sign-on, CRM integrations, and a centralized admin dashboard with team analytics for larger deployments. Krisp positions its on-device processing architecture as a privacy advantage relative to cloud-first transcription tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Krisp uses a four-tier freemium model. The Free plan includes 60 minutes per day of noise cancellation but no transcription, summaries, or recording. The Pro plan, at $8 per month, removes the daily noise-cancellation limit and adds unlimited AI summaries, action items, meeting history, and recording. The Business plan, at $15 per month, layers on CRM integrations, SSO, expanded storage, and team management, while Enterprise pricing is negotiated and adds centralized billing, organization-wide analytics, and custom security policies.

A seven-day free trial unlocks all paid functionality, including unlimited transcription and recording, letting prospective customers evaluate the full feature set before committing to Pro, Business, or Enterprise.

features.title: AI Noise Cancellation
features.description: Removes background noise and echo from both microphone and speaker audio in real time using on-device deep learning models.

features.title: Background Voice Cancellation
features.description: Filters out other people talking in the same room so only the primary speaker's voice comes through on a call.

features.title: AI Meeting Assistant
features.description: Joins or listens to calls to produce live transcripts, AI-generated summaries, and action items.

features.title: Meeting Recording and History
features.description: Records audio and video of calls and stores a searchable meeting history in one place.

features.title: App-Agnostic Integration
features.description: Works as a virtual microphone and speaker layer with Zoom, Google Meet, Microsoft Teams, Slack huddles, and most VoIP or call center software.

features.title: On-Device Privacy Architecture
features.description: Processes audio locally on the device rather than streaming it to the cloud, supporting HIPAA and GDPR compliant use on paid plans.

features.title: CRM and Business Integrations
features.description: Business plan adds CRM integrations, SSO, and expanded storage for team-wide deployment.

features.title: Enterprise Admin Controls
features.description: Centralized admin dashboard, organization-wide analytics, and custom security policies for large deployments.

pros: Effective real-time noise and voice cancellation that works across virtually any calling app
Free tier is genuinely usable for individuals with a daily minute allowance
On-device processing model appeals to privacy- and compliance-conscious users
AI meeting summaries and action items reduce manual note-taking
Lightweight desktop app with a low learning curve
cons: Free plan is capped at 60 minutes of noise cancellation per day
Transcription and AI meeting features require a paid Pro or Business plan
Enterprise tier has no published price and requires a sales conversation
Primarily an audio and meeting-intelligence layer rather than a full conferencing replacement
Desktop app must run continuously in the background to filter audio

faqs.question: What does Krisp actually do?
faqs.answer: Krisp is a desktop and mobile app that sits between your microphone and speakers and any calling app, using on-device AI to strip out background noise, echo, and other people's voices, while its AI meeting assistant can transcribe calls and generate summaries.

faqs.question: Is Krisp free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Krisp offers a free plan with 60 minutes of noise cancellation per day; unlimited noise cancellation and AI meeting features require the Pro plan starting at $8/month.

faqs.question: Does Krisp work with Zoom, Google Meet, and Microsoft Teams?
faqs.answer: Yes, Krisp is app-agnostic and works with virtually any conferencing or calling software, including Zoom, Google Meet, Microsoft Teams, and Slack huddles.

faqs.question: Is my audio sent to Krisp's servers?
faqs.answer: Krisp markets its noise cancellation as processed locally on-device, which is part of why it supports HIPAA and GDPR compliant use on paid plans.

faqs.question: Who founded Krisp and when?
faqs.answer: Krisp was founded in 2017 by Davit Baghdasaryan and Artavazd Minasyan.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Krisp?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Otter.ai, Fireflies.ai, Fathom, and Nyota, though most of these focus primarily on transcription rather than Krisp's core strength of audio noise cancellation.

use_cases.title: Remote and hybrid team meetings
use_cases.description: Eliminates keyboard clicks, dogs barking, traffic, and other household noise during video calls.

use_cases.title: Sales and support call centers
use_cases.description: Cleans up agent and customer audio while producing call summaries and searchable meeting history for coaching and CRM logging.
use_cases.audience: Sales reps and customer support teams

use_cases.title: Regulated industries
use_cases.description: Business and Enterprise tiers support HIPAA and GDPR-aligned handling of call audio, plus SSO and admin controls for organization-wide deployment.
use_cases.audience: Compliance-conscious enterprises in healthcare, legal, and finance

features: Unlimited AI summaries and action items

enrichment.short_description: Krita is a free, open-source digital painting and 2D animation program built for illustrators, concept artists, and comic creators.
enrichment.seo_title: Krita Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Krita review: a free open-source video editor with multi-track timelines, color scopes and speech-to-text. See features, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Krita is a free and open-source digital painting and 2D animation application whose origins trace back to a presentation by KDE co-founder Matthias Ettrich at the 1998 Linux Kongress, after which members of the KDE community started building a dedicated image editor for the KOffice suite, originally named KImage and then KImageShop, with the project formally kicked off by a mailing-list message from Matthias Elter on May 31, 1999. Trademark conflicts forced two renames, first to Krayon and then, in 2002, to Krita, the name it has carried ever since; the first public release shipped as part of KOffice 1.4 in 2004. In 2012, the Krita community formalized a nonprofit, the Krita Foundation, based in Deventer, Netherlands, to fund and coordinate ongoing development; Dmitry Kazakov became the foundation's first sponsored developer and remains Krita's lead developer today. Architecturally, Krita is a raster-based painting application with an OpenGL-accelerated canvas, built around an unusually deep brush engine system: it ships with more than a dozen distinct brush engines (pixel, smudge, particle, shape, texture, and more) that support pressure and tilt sensitivity for graphics tablets, alongside colour management spanning RGB, CMYK, and HDR colour spaces, features that are uncommon or first-of-their-kind among free painting software. Beyond painting, Krita includes non-destructive layers, layer groups, and masks; vector layers for shapes and text alongside raster content in the same document; a full animation module with a frame-by-frame timeline, onion skinning, and export to GIF, WebM, and image sequences; painting assistants such as perspective grids and vanishing-point rulers aimed at technical and comic artists; and a Python scripting API that lets advanced users automate tasks and build custom plugins and dockers. The software runs on Windows 8.1 and later, macOS, Linux (via distribution packages or AppImage), and Android/ChromeOS. Licensing is the defining fact of Krita's business model: the software is released under the GNU General Public License version 3, meaning it can always be downloaded, used, modified, and redistributed for free directly from krita.org, with no subscription, trial period, premium tier, or feature paywall of any kind. To generate revenue for continued development, the Krita Foundation also distributes the identical software through storefronts such as Steam, the Microsoft Store, and Google Play at a modest price point (historically around $9.99, sometimes discounted), functioning as a pay-what-you-want-style donation rather than a separate paid edition; the foundation additionally accepts direct donations. Krita's target audience is digital illustrators, concept artists for games and film, comic and webcomic creators, and hobbyist or professional 2D animators who want professional-grade painting tools without a subscription cost. Its most direct competitors are commercial subscription tools like Adobe Photoshop and the tablet-native app Procreate (iPad-only, one-time purchase), as well as fellow open-source project GIMP; Krita differentiates itself from GIMP by being purpose-built for painting and illustration workflows with tablet-first brush engines and animation tooling, rather than GIMP's general-purpose photo-editing focus, and differentiates itself from Photoshop and Procreate primarily on cost and licensing freedom, since Krita has no recurring fee and its source code is fully open for audit and modification. The tradeoffs are that Krita lacks built-in cloud collaboration or sync comparable to some commercial suites, has no official iOS or iPad app, and, as a community- and donation-funded nonprofit project, depends on foundation funding and volunteer contribution for its release cadence rather than a dedicated commercial engineering organization.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Krita is a free and open-source raster graphics editor purpose-built for digital painting, illustration, and 2D animation. Its roots go back to 1999 as a KDE community project called KImageShop, which was renamed twice for trademark reasons before settling on the name Krita in 2002, with the first public release arriving as part of KOffice 1.4 in 2004.

Since 2012, development has been coordinated by the nonprofit Krita Foundation in Deventer, Netherlands, led by lead developer Dmitry Kazakov. The software is licensed under the GNU General Public License v3, so it is always free to download, use, and modify, with paid copies on stores like Steam functioning as optional donations rather than a separate paid tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Krita's core strength is its brush engine system, which includes more than a dozen engines (pixel, smudge, particle, shape, and texture, among others) with full pressure and tilt sensitivity for graphics tablets, plus wide colour-space support including CMYK and HDR that is uncommon among free painting tools. Non-destructive layers, layer groups, masks, and combined vector-and-raster editing round out its core painting toolkit.

Beyond static illustration, Krita ships a complete animation module with a frame-by-frame timeline, onion skinning, and export to GIF, WebM, and image sequences, plus painting assistants like perspective grids for technical and comic art. A built-in Python scripting API allows advanced users to automate workflows and build custom plugins.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Krita is entirely free under the GPL v3 license when downloaded directly from krita.org, with no subscription, trial period, or feature paywall of any kind. The full, identical application can also be purchased on storefronts like Steam, the Microsoft Store, and Google Play for a modest one-time price, historically around $9.99.

These storefront purchases function as an optional way to support the Krita Foundation's development work and receive automatic updates through the store, not as a separate paid edition with extra features.

features.title: Advanced Brush Engine
features.description: Dozens of customizable brush engines, including pixel, smudge, particle, and shape engines, with pressure and tilt sensitivity for pen tablets.

features.title: Non-Destructive Layers and Masks
features.description: Group-based layer management, layer styles, clone layers, and filter or transform masks.

features.title: Animation Timeline
features.description: Frame-by-frame animation docker with onion skinning and export to GIF, WebM, and image sequences.

features.title: Vector and Text Tools
features.description: Vector layers for shapes and text alongside raster painting in the same document.

features.title: Wide Color Management
features.description: Support for RGB, CMYK, and HDR color spaces for print and high-dynamic-range work.

features.title: Python Scripting and Plugins
features.description: A scripting API that lets users automate tasks and build custom plugins and dockers.

features.title: Painting Assistants
features.description: Perspective grids, rulers, and vanishing-point guides for technical and comic art.

features.title: Resource Bundles
features.description: Bundle-based sharing of brushes, palettes, gradients, and templates between artists.

pros: Completely free with no subscriptions, watermarks, or feature paywalls
Professional-grade brush engine rivals paid competitors like Photoshop and Procreate
Fully open source under GPL v3 so the code can be audited, modified, and redistributed
Built-in animation tools remove the need for a separate 2D animation app
Active community and frequent releases funded by a nonprofit foundation
cons: No built-in cloud collaboration or sync comparable to some commercial apps
Steeper learning curve for beginners compared to simplified apps like Procreate
No official iOS or iPad app, only Android/ChromeOS on mobile
Relies on donations and optional store purchases, so development pace depends on funding
Advanced typography and print-production features lag behind Adobe Illustrator or Photoshop

faqs.question: Is Krita really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Krita is completely free and open source under the GNU General Public License v3; the versions sold on Steam, the Microsoft Store, or Google Play are optional paid copies that fund development, but the identical software is always free at krita.org.

faqs.question: What is Krita mainly used for?
faqs.answer: Krita is used for digital painting, illustration, concept art, comics, and 2D frame-by-frame animation.

faqs.question: Does Krita run on Mac and Linux?
faqs.answer: Yes, Krita runs on Windows, macOS, Linux (via distribution packages or AppImage), and Android/ChromeOS.

faqs.question: Who develops Krita?
faqs.answer: Krita is developed by a community of contributors coordinated by the nonprofit Krita Foundation, based in Deventer, Netherlands, with Dmitry Kazakov as lead developer.

faqs.question: How is Krita different from GIMP or Photoshop?
faqs.answer: Unlike GIMP, which is a general-purpose image editor, Krita is purpose-built for painting and illustration with tablet-focused brush engines and animation tools; unlike Photoshop, it is free, open source, and has no subscription.

faqs.question: Does Krita support Python scripting?
faqs.answer: Yes, Krita includes a Python scripting API that lets users automate tasks and build custom plugins and dockers.

use_cases.title: Digital illustration and concept art
use_cases.description: Professional brush engine and layer tools for painting original artwork and game or film concept art.

use_cases.title: Comic and webcomic creation
use_cases.description: Vector text, panel templates, and perspective assistants streamline comic page production.

use_cases.title: 2D animation
use_cases.description: Built-in timeline, onion skinning, and export to GIF, WebM, and image sequences support short-form frame-by-frame animation without extra software.

features: Available on krita.org for Windows, macOS, and Linux

pricing_plans.plan_name: Store Version (Steam, Microsoft Store, Google Play)
features: Identical software to the free download
Purchase price supports Krita Foundation development

enrichment.short_description: Kroki is a free, open-source API that renders text-based markup into diagrams, supporting over 20 diagram types through one unified endpoint.
enrichment.seo_title: Kroki Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kroki review: the free, open-source diagrams-as-code API's supported diagram types, self-hosting, integrations, and how it compares to alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Kroki is a free and open-source unified API for generating diagrams from plain-text markup, created by French software engineer Guillaume Grossetie and published under the GitHub organization yuzutech, with the project first launched publicly in April 2019. Kroki is released under the MIT license and has grown into a community project with thousands of GitHub stars and dozens of contributors. The core idea behind Kroki is to solve the fragmentation problem in diagrams-as-code tooling: rather than installing and maintaining separate renderers for each diagram syntax a team might want to use, Kroki exposes a single HTTP API that accepts text written in any of more than 20 supported diagram languages and returns a rendered image. Supported diagram types include widely used options such as Mermaid, PlantUML, GraphViz, BPMN, C4, Ditaa, Excalidraw, Vega and Vega-Lite, WaveDrom, Nomnoml, Pikchr, SvgBob, UMLet, Symbolator, DBML, Bytefield, BlockDiag, Erd, and Diagrams.net (draw.io), among others, with new diagram types added over time. Architecturally, Kroki supports both GET and POST requests: for GET requests, the diagram source is compressed with deflate and base64-encoded directly into the URL path in the form of /{diagram_type}/{output_format}/{encoded_diagram}, which makes it easy to embed live diagram links in documentation or Markdown files, while POST requests allow larger raw diagram text to be submitted directly in the request body. Output formats vary by diagram type but commonly include SVG, PNG, PDF, and JPEG. Kroki's architecture is deliberately modular: diagram types that depend on third-party rendering engines, such as Excalidraw, Structurizr, or Pikchr, run as separate companion microservices rather than being baked into the core Kroki server, which lets the project add support for new diagram tools without destabilizing the core system. Because Kroki is open source and ships as Docker images, including the companion service containers, organizations that need to keep proprietary architecture or sequence diagrams off third-party infrastructure can self-host the entire stack behind their own firewall using Docker or Docker Compose. For users who don't need to self-host, the project also operates a free public instance at kroki.io that anyone can call directly without installing anything, making it simple to prototype or use Kroki in low-volume personal or open-source projects. Kroki has built an ecosystem of official and community integrations that embed its rendering directly into documentation tooling, including plugins for Asciidoctor, MkDocs, and Confluence (via a dedicated Kroki Diagrams for Confluence marketplace app), letting technical writers and developers keep diagrams as version-controlled text alongside their documentation rather than maintaining separate binary image files that drift out of sync with the underlying system they describe. Because Kroki has no company behind it in the traditional commercial sense, it does not have named pricing tiers, funding rounds, or a formal headquarters; it is entirely free to use, whether through the public API or a self-hosted deployment, and third-party managed hosting providers such as Elestio offer paid, fully-managed Kroki instances for teams that want the self-hosted assurance without operating the infrastructure themselves. Kroki's primary competitors are the individual diagram renderers it wraps and orchestrates, such as running PlantUML, Mermaid, or GraphViz directly via their own native command-line tools or dedicated hosted services, as well as commercial diagramming platforms like Lucidchart, draw.io/diagrams.net, and Mermaid Live/Mermaid Chart; Kroki differentiates itself from these by being diagram-syntax agnostic and API-first, giving developers and technical writers a single consistent integration point regardless of which text-based diagram language a project or team standardizes on, rather than locking them into one specific diagram syntax or a proprietary visual editor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kroki is a free, open-source API that converts plain-text diagram descriptions into rendered images. Created by Guillaume Grossetie under the yuzutech GitHub organization and first published in April 2019, it is released under the MIT license and has grown through community contributions into a project supporting more than 20 diagram-as-code languages.

Rather than requiring separate tooling for each diagram syntax, Kroki exposes one unified HTTP endpoint that accepts markup in languages like Mermaid, PlantUML, or GraphViz and returns SVG, PNG, PDF, or JPEG output, making it a common building block behind documentation sites and internal developer tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Kroki supports GET requests with deflate-compressed, base64-encoded diagram source embedded directly in the URL, ideal for linking live diagrams from Markdown or wiki pages, as well as POST requests for larger diagram text. Its modular architecture runs diagram types that need external engines, such as Excalidraw or Structurizr, as separate companion microservices, which keeps the core service lean while still supporting a wide range of syntaxes.

Because Kroki ships as Docker images, teams can self-host it entirely on their own infrastructure to keep sensitive architecture diagrams private, or use the free public instance at kroki.io for quick, no-install access. Official integrations with tools like Asciidoctor, MkDocs, and Confluence let teams embed diagrams-as-code directly into their existing documentation workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Kroki itself is entirely free, both as the publicly hosted kroki.io API and as a self-hosted Docker deployment, since it is an open-source, MIT-licensed community project rather than a commercial product with pricing tiers.

Teams that want a fully managed, self-hosted-equivalent instance without operating the Docker infrastructure themselves can turn to third-party managed hosting providers, such as Elestio, which offer paid hosting plans for Kroki as one of many open-source tools in their catalog.

features.title: Unified Diagram API
features.description: A single HTTP endpoint renders 20+ text-based diagram languages, including Mermaid, PlantUML, GraphViz, BPMN, C4, Excalidraw, and Vega-Lite.

features.title: GET and POST Endpoints
features.description: Supports lightweight GET requests with deflate-compressed, base64-encoded diagram source in the URL, and POST requests with raw diagram text for larger diagrams.

features.title: Multiple Output Formats
features.description: Renders diagrams to SVG, PNG, PDF, or JPEG depending on the diagram type.

features.title: Self-Hosted Docker Deployment
features.description: Ships as Docker images, including companion service containers, so teams can run Kroki entirely on their own infrastructure.

features.title: Free Public Instance
features.description: kroki.io hosts a free public API so users can try or use the service without installing anything.

features.title: Documentation Tool Integrations
features.description: Official plugins for tools like Asciidoctor, MkDocs, and Confluence to embed diagrams directly in docs.

features.title: Modular Companion Services
features.description: Diagram types that need external tools, such as Excalidraw, Structurizr, or Pikchr, run as separate companion microservices for easy extension.

features.title: MIT-Licensed Open Source
features.description: Source hosted on GitHub under the MIT license with active community contributions.

pros: Free to use, both as a self-hosted service and via the public kroki.io API
One API covers dozens of diagram-as-code syntaxes instead of installing separate renderers
Docker-based self-hosting keeps proprietary diagrams off third-party servers
Wide ecosystem of plugins for documentation tools like Asciidoctor, MkDocs, and Confluence
Actively maintained open-source project with a permissive MIT license
cons: No official SaaS support contract or SLA, since it is a community open-source project
Self-hosting the full set of diagram types requires running multiple companion containers
Complex diagrams can render slowly on the free public instance
No built-in visual, WYSIWYG diagram editor, since input is text-based markup
Documentation assumes familiarity with the underlying diagram syntaxes, such as PlantUML or Mermaid

faqs.question: What is Kroki?
faqs.answer: Kroki is a free, open-source API that converts plain-text diagram descriptions, written in languages like Mermaid, PlantUML, or GraphViz, into rendered images such as SVG or PNG.

faqs.question: Do I need to install anything to use Kroki?
faqs.answer: No, you can call the free public API at kroki.io directly, or self-host Kroki with Docker if you need full control over your infrastructure.

faqs.question: Which diagram types does Kroki support?
faqs.answer: Kroki supports more than 20 diagram types, including Mermaid, PlantUML, GraphViz, BPMN, C4, Excalidraw, Ditaa, Vega, Vega-Lite, WaveDrom, and Diagrams.net (draw.io).

faqs.question: Is Kroki free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kroki is completely free and open source under the MIT license, both for the hosted public instance and for self-hosted deployments.

faqs.question: Who maintains Kroki?
faqs.answer: Kroki is maintained under the GitHub organization yuzutech, created by Guillaume Grossetie, and was first published in 2019.

faqs.question: Can Kroki be embedded in documentation tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kroki has integrations for tools such as Asciidoctor, MkDocs, and Confluence, letting teams embed diagrams-as-code directly in their docs.

use_cases.title: Docs-as-code diagramming
use_cases.description: Embed version-controlled, text-based diagrams directly in Markdown or AsciiDoc documentation instead of maintaining binary image files.

use_cases.title: Internal developer platforms
use_cases.description: Self-host Kroki behind a firewall so architecture and sequence diagrams never leave internal infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Automated diagram generation in CI/CD
use_cases.description: Call the Kroki API from build pipelines to regenerate architecture or pipeline diagrams automatically whenever source text changes.

features: Access to all supported diagram types

enrichment.short_description: Kubeflow is an open-source machine learning toolkit that runs training, tuning, pipeline orchestration, and model serving natively on Kubernetes.
enrichment.seo_title: Kubeflow Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kubeflow review: the open-source Kubernetes MLOps toolkit's components, CNCF status, self-hosting, and how it compares to other ML platforms.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Kubeflow is an open-source machine learning toolkit for building, training, tuning, and deploying models as reproducible, containerized workflows on Kubernetes. The project originated inside Google, where engineers were running TensorFlow training jobs at scale on Kubernetes and wanted a more general, reusable way to package that pattern; Kubeflow was publicly announced in 2017 at KubeCon plus CloudNativeCon North America by Google engineers David Aronchick, Jeremy Lewi, and Vishnu Kannan, in response to a perceived lack of flexible, production-ready options for building machine learning systems on top of Kubernetes. Over the following years, Kubeflow grew from a Google-led initiative into a broad multi-vendor community effort, with the Kubeflow community eventually including contributors from over 150 companies and delivering more than fifteen major releases. Reflecting that shift toward a vendor-neutral governance model, Google applied to move Kubeflow into the Cloud Native Computing Foundation in October 2022, and the CNCF's Technical Oversight Committee formally accepted Kubeflow as an incubating project in July 2023, placing it in the same governance structure as Kubernetes itself, Prometheus, and other widely adopted cloud-native infrastructure projects. Architecturally, Kubeflow is not a single monolithic application but a curated collection of Kubernetes-native components, each addressing a different stage of the machine learning lifecycle. Kubeflow Notebooks provide hosted, multi-user Jupyter notebook environments running directly inside the cluster for interactive model development. Kubeflow Pipelines is a workflow orchestration engine for defining, running, and versioning multi-step, containerized ML pipelines, so that a full training and evaluation process can be re-run reproducibly rather than existing only as an ad hoc notebook. The Training Operator provides Kubernetes custom resources and controllers for running distributed training jobs across popular frameworks including TensorFlow, PyTorch, XGBoost, and Apache MXNet, letting a job span multiple nodes and GPUs using native Kubernetes scheduling. Katib is Kubeflow's automated hyperparameter tuning and neural architecture search component, running many training trials in parallel to find optimal model configurations. KServe (previously known as KFServing) is the standardized, autoscaling model-serving component used to deploy trained models as production inference endpoints, supporting canary rollouts and multiple ML frameworks out of the box. A central web dashboard ties these components together and, combined with namespace-based multi-tenancy, lets multiple data science teams share one underlying Kubernetes cluster with logical isolation between their notebooks, pipelines, and experiments. Because Kubeflow is entirely open source and distributed as Kubernetes manifests and Helm-style deployment tooling, there is no purchase price or subscription: the software itself is free, and the only ongoing cost is the underlying Kubernetes infrastructure it runs on, whether that is a self-managed on-premises cluster or a managed Kubernetes service on a cloud provider such as Google Kubernetes Engine, Amazon EKS, or Azure Kubernetes Service. Kubeflow's target users are ML platform engineering teams and data science organizations that want a standardized, portable, and reproducible way for many practitioners to train and deploy models without locking themselves into a single cloud vendor's proprietary ML platform. Its most direct competitors are cloud-native managed ML platforms such as Amazon SageMaker, Google Vertex AI, and Azure Machine Learning, as well as other open-source MLOps tools such as MLflow and Metaflow; Kubeflow differentiates itself from the managed cloud platforms primarily on portability and openness, since it can run identically on any conformant Kubernetes cluster across clouds or on-premises rather than tying an organization to one vendor's proprietary APIs and billing, while its tradeoff relative to managed platforms is that it requires an organization to operate and secure its own Kubernetes-based ML infrastructure rather than consuming it as a fully managed service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kubeflow is an open-source toolkit for running the machine learning lifecycle, from notebooks to distributed training to hyperparameter tuning to model serving, as Kubernetes-native, containerized workflows. It was created in 2017 by Google engineers David Aronchick, Jeremy Lewi, and Vishnu Kannan to generalize the internal pattern Google used for running TensorFlow at scale on Kubernetes.

Kubeflow has since grown into a broad, multi-vendor community project with contributors from more than 150 companies, and in July 2023 the CNCF accepted it as an incubating project, placing its governance alongside Kubernetes, Prometheus, and other major cloud-native infrastructure projects.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Kubeflow is organized as a set of composable components: Kubeflow Notebooks for hosted, multi-user Jupyter environments; Kubeflow Pipelines for defining and versioning reproducible, multi-step ML workflows; the Training Operator for distributed training across TensorFlow, PyTorch, XGBoost, and other frameworks; Katib for automated hyperparameter tuning and neural architecture search; and KServe for standardized, autoscaling model serving with canary rollout support.

A central dashboard and namespace-based multi-tenancy let multiple data science teams share a single Kubernetes cluster with logical isolation, and because every component runs as native Kubernetes resources, the same Kubeflow deployment can run identically on-premises or across any major cloud provider's Kubernetes service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Kubeflow itself carries no license fee or subscription cost, since it is fully open source and distributed as Kubernetes manifests and deployment tooling that any team can install on their own cluster.

The real cost of running Kubeflow comes from the underlying Kubernetes infrastructure and compute (CPUs, GPUs, and storage) it consumes, plus the engineering time required to operate and secure the cluster, which is why many organizations use a managed Kubernetes service or a commercial Kubeflow distribution to reduce operational overhead.

Framework-agnostic, supporting TensorFlow, PyTorch, and other major libraries
Backed by a large multi-company community and now a CNCF incubating project
Reproducible, containerized pipelines improve collaboration and auditability
cons: Steep setup and operational complexity, requiring solid Kubernetes expertise
Frequent component changes between releases can complicate long-term upgrades
Resource-heavy to run compared to lighter-weight ML pipeline tools
Documentation and UX vary in polish across the many sub-projects
No commercial support included; enterprises typically need a managed distribution or in-house platform team

faqs.question: What is Kubeflow used for?
faqs.answer: Kubeflow is used to build, train, tune, and deploy machine learning models as reproducible, containerized workflows running on Kubernetes.

faqs.question: Who created Kubeflow?
faqs.answer: Kubeflow was created in 2017 by Google engineers, including David Aronchick, Jeremy Lewi, and Vishnu Kannan, originally to run TensorFlow workloads at scale on Kubernetes.

faqs.question: Is Kubeflow free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kubeflow is fully open source and free to use; costs come from the underlying Kubernetes infrastructure it runs on.

faqs.question: Does Kubeflow require Kubernetes expertise?
faqs.answer: Yes, deploying and operating Kubeflow assumes familiarity with Kubernetes concepts like pods, custom resources, and namespaces.

faqs.question: Is Kubeflow part of the CNCF?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kubeflow was accepted as a CNCF incubating project in July 2023.

faqs.question: What ML frameworks does Kubeflow support?
faqs.answer: Kubeflow supports TensorFlow, PyTorch, XGBoost, Apache MXNet, and other popular machine learning frameworks through its training operators.

enrichment.short_description: KubeVirt is an open-source, CNCF-incubating project that lets Kubernetes clusters run and manage traditional virtual machines alongside containers.
enrichment.seo_title: KubeVirt Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: KubeVirt review: the open-source CNCF project for running VMs on Kubernetes, its architecture, live migration, and how it compares to alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: KubeVirt is an open-source project that extends Kubernetes so that clusters can run and manage traditional, KVM-based virtual machines using the same declarative API and tooling used for containers. The project began at Red Hat at the end of 2016 and was formally started in January 2017, prompted by the question of whether virtual machines could be run inside containers and deployed by Kubernetes, so that organizations with existing VM-based workloads would not need to fully rewrite them as containers to benefit from Kubernetes-native scheduling, networking, and storage. KubeVirt was accepted into the Cloud Native Computing Foundation as a Sandbox project on September 6, 2019, and was promoted to Incubating maturity level on April 19, 2022, and version 1.0 shipped in mid-2023, milestones that reflect its maturing production use. Since joining the CNCF, the project has attracted contributors well beyond its Red Hat origins, including engineers from Amadeus, Apple, CloudFlare, Containership, Giant Swarm, Gitpod, IBM, Kubermatic, NEC, NVIDIA, SAP, SUSE, and independent developers. Architecturally, KubeVirt extends the Kubernetes API by introducing new custom resource definitions, most importantly VirtualMachine, VirtualMachineInstance, and VirtualMachineInstanceReplicaSet, which let a VM be created, updated, and deleted using the same declarative, kubectl-based workflow used for any other Kubernetes object. Under the hood, each virtual machine actually runs inside a Kubernetes pod that contains a containerized QEMU process managed by libvirt, with the Linux kernel's KVM (Kernel-based Virtual Machine) module providing the underlying hardware-assisted hypervisor; a primary controller called virt-controller, running as a normal Kubernetes pod, manages the lifecycle of these VM resources across the cluster. A companion command-line tool called virtctl works alongside the standard kubectl CLI to provide VM-specific operations such as starting, stopping, restarting, and pausing virtual machines, managing disk images, and opening the VM's serial, graphical (VNC), or SSH console. Because VMs are ordinary Kubernetes objects, they integrate directly with the cluster's existing storage classes and CSI drivers for persistent, stateful storage, and with standard Kubernetes CNI networking plugins and network policies, so VM workloads share the same storage and networking model as containerized workloads rather than requiring a separate virtualization stack. One of KubeVirt's most important capabilities is live migration: a running VM and its memory state can be moved from one cluster node to another without interrupting the applications running on it, using a pre-copy migration process in which the source node streams memory pages to the destination while the VM keeps running, resending any pages that change ('dirty' pages) until the remaining set is small enough for a fast final switchover; live migration can be triggered declaratively by creating a VirtualMachineInstanceMigration custom resource or by running the virtctl migrate command, and is commonly used for planned hardware maintenance, load balancing, or responding to node failures. As a CNCF-governed open-source project distributed under the Apache 2.0 license, KubeVirt itself carries no license fee: it is free to download and run on any Kubernetes cluster with nodes that support hardware virtualization (KVM), and community support is available through the CNCF Slack and GitHub. Organizations that want commercial support typically adopt KubeVirt indirectly through a vendor distribution, most notably Red Hat OpenShift Virtualization, which packages KubeVirt with enterprise support, a management UI, and additional tooling; other Kubernetes platform vendors, including SUSE and various Kubernetes-as-a-service providers, have also built virtualization offerings on top of KubeVirt. KubeVirt's primary competitors and alternatives are traditional hypervisor platforms such as VMware vSphere and its VM-management layer, as well as other Kubernetes-based virtualization approaches; its key differentiator is that it lets an organization consolidate VM and container operations onto a single Kubernetes control plane and toolchain, rather than running a separate hypervisor stack alongside Kubernetes, which is especially valuable for teams migrating legacy, non-containerized applications into a cloud-native environment without a full rewrite.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: KubeVirt is an open-source project that lets Kubernetes clusters run and manage traditional virtual machines using the same declarative API and command-line tooling used for containers. It began at Red Hat at the end of 2016 and formally launched in January 2017, aimed at letting organizations run VM-based workloads on Kubernetes without first rewriting them as containers.

The project joined the CNCF as a Sandbox project in September 2019 and reached Incubating maturity in April 2022, with version 1.0 shipping in 2023. It now has contributors from companies including Red Hat, Apple, NVIDIA, SUSE, IBM, and SAP, and underpins commercial products such as Red Hat OpenShift Virtualization.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: KubeVirt extends the Kubernetes API with VirtualMachine and VirtualMachineInstance custom resources, letting a VM be created, updated, and deleted with the same kubectl-based workflow used for any other Kubernetes object. Under the hood, each VM runs inside a pod containing a containerized QEMU process managed by libvirt on top of the Linux kernel's KVM hypervisor, while a companion CLI called virtctl adds VM-specific commands like start, stop, pause, console access, and image management alongside standard kubectl.

Because VMs are ordinary Kubernetes resources, they integrate directly with existing storage classes, CSI drivers, and CNI networking plugins. Live migration lets a running VM move between nodes with no or minimal downtime using pre-copy memory migration, useful for planned maintenance, load balancing, or responding to node failures.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: KubeVirt is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, with no cost to download and run on any Kubernetes cluster whose nodes support hardware virtualization (KVM). Community support is available through the CNCF Slack and GitHub.

Organizations that want commercial support generally adopt KubeVirt through a vendor distribution, most notably Red Hat OpenShift Virtualization, which bundles KubeVirt with enterprise support, a management UI, and additional operational tooling.

features.title: VirtualMachine CRDs

features.title: Live Migration
features.description: Moves running VMs between nodes without downtime using pre-copy memory migration, for maintenance, load balancing, or node failure response.

features.title: virtctl CLI
features.description: A companion to kubectl for VM-specific actions like start, stop, pause, console access, and image management.

features.title: KVM/QEMU-Based Virtualization
features.description: Runs each VM inside a pod using containerized QEMU and libvirt on top of the Linux KVM hypervisor.

features.title: Storage and CSI Integration
features.description: Attaches persistent volumes and Kubernetes storage classes to VMs for stateful virtual machine workloads.

features.title: Networking via Kubernetes CNI
features.description: VMs share the same network plugins and policies used by containerized workloads in the cluster.

features.title: Mixed Container and VM Workloads
features.description: Lets legacy, non-containerized applications run alongside cloud-native microservices in the same cluster.

features.title: CNCF-Governed Project
features.description: Incubating CNCF project with contributors from Red Hat, NVIDIA, SUSE, IBM, and others.

pros: Lets teams run legacy VM workloads on the same Kubernetes control plane as containers, avoiding a separate hypervisor stack
Free, open source, and governed by the CNCF with broad multi-vendor contribution
Live migration and VM lifecycle management match many capabilities of traditional hypervisors
Uses the same kubectl-based tooling and RBAC as the rest of the cluster
Underpins commercial products like Red Hat OpenShift Virtualization, so it has real production mileage
cons: Requires nodes with hardware virtualization (KVM) support, which not all cloud instances offer by default
Adds operational complexity on top of an already complex Kubernetes stack
Feature parity with mature hypervisors like VMware vSphere is still catching up in some areas
Community support only unless paired with a commercial distribution such as OpenShift Virtualization
VM performance tuning and storage configuration require deeper virtualization knowledge than typical container workloads

faqs.question: What is KubeVirt used for?
faqs.answer: KubeVirt lets Kubernetes clusters run and manage traditional virtual machines alongside containers, which is useful for migrating legacy VM-based applications to a cloud-native platform.

faqs.question: Who started KubeVirt?
faqs.answer: KubeVirt was started at Red Hat around the end of 2016 and formally launched in January 2017.

faqs.question: Is KubeVirt free?
faqs.answer: Yes, KubeVirt is a free, open-source CNCF project; commercial support is available through vendors like Red Hat via products such as OpenShift Virtualization.

faqs.question: Does KubeVirt support live migration?
faqs.answer: Yes, KubeVirt supports live migration of running VMs between cluster nodes with minimal to no downtime.

faqs.question: What hypervisor does KubeVirt use under the hood?
faqs.answer: KubeVirt runs QEMU and libvirt inside containers, using the Linux kernel's KVM module as the underlying hypervisor.

faqs.question: Is KubeVirt a CNCF project?
faqs.answer: Yes, KubeVirt joined the CNCF as a Sandbox project in September 2019 and reached Incubating status in April 2022.

use_cases.description: Spin up and tear down full VMs on demand for test labs, CI environments, or virtual desktops using Kubernetes-native tooling.

Community support via CNCF Slack and GitHub

enrichment.short_description: Kudos is an employee recognition and rewards platform that helps organizations build peer-to-peer appreciation, engagement, and performance insights.
enrichment.seo_title: Kudos Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kudos review 2026: the employee recognition and rewards platform's features, pricing, integrations, and how it compares to Bonusly and Nectar.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Kudos is an employee recognition and rewards platform built to help organizations create a culture of appreciation that drives engagement, performance, and connection across a workforce. The company is headquartered in Calgary, Alberta, Canada, with sources placing its founding around 2010 (though some third-party databases list slightly different founding years, ranging roughly from 2007 to 2015), and has since expanded with additional offices supporting operations in the United States and Sweden. Reported company size places Kudos at roughly 90 employees, and the company raised 10 million dollars in growth funding in 2022 as part of a push to double its Calgary-based workforce, reflecting continued investor interest in the employee engagement software category. At its core, Kudos is built around a live, social-style recognition feed where employees and managers publicly give and receive recognition, and a defining design choice is that every piece of recognition must be tied to one of the organization's defined company values, which nudges people toward more specific, thoughtful praise rather than generic thanks and gives leadership a running record of which values actually show up in day-to-day work rather than just appearing on a poster in the breakroom. Beyond peer-to-peer recognition, the platform supports milestone and work-anniversary celebrations delivered as virtual cards, manager-driven award programs, structured nomination campaigns for spotlighting top performers, and configurable incentive programs designed to reinforce specific target behaviors across teams. An optional rewards catalog lets organizations layer a points-based redemption system, such as gift cards or merchandise, on top of the core social recognition feed for companies that want tangible rewards attached to recognition rather than social acknowledgment alone. On the analytics side, Kudos provides a people analytics dashboard that gives HR and leadership visibility into recognition volume, participation rates, and engagement trends broken down by team and by which company values are being reinforced, positioning the product as a data-driven culture and engagement tool rather than a simple digital thank-you-card app. The platform integrates with collaboration tools employees already use daily, including Microsoft Teams and Slack, as well as major HR information systems and marketplaces such as ADP and UKG, letting recognition workflows plug into existing communication and HR tech stacks rather than becoming a disconnected, rarely visited destination site; a mobile app extends recognition and rewards access beyond the desktop for distributed and frontline workers. On pricing, Kudos does not publish fixed tiers or list prices publicly; instead the company uses a custom subscription quote based on organization size, feature set, and whether the optional rewards catalog is included, typically billed monthly or annually. One independently reported real-world data point puts the standard platform cost at around three dollars per employee per month, though actual pricing will vary by contract and company size, and prospective buyers are generally directed to request a demo and custom quote rather than seeing self-serve pricing on the company's website. Kudos's target audience is HR and People teams at small-to-mid-size and larger organizations that want a structured, values-aligned recognition program rather than ad hoc praise, as well as individual people managers running regular recognition or nomination campaigns for their teams, and organizations with significant remote or distributed workforces that need a shared digital space to keep appreciation visible across locations. In the competitive landscape, Kudos sits alongside other employee recognition and engagement platforms such as Bonusly, Nectar, Motivosity, Achievers, and Workhuman; Kudos differentiates itself primarily through its values-tagging design, which explicitly links every recognition moment to a specific organizational value and rolls that data up into leadership-facing analytics, positioning it as more of a configurable culture-measurement tool than a purely social kudos wall, while competitors such as Bonusly lean more heavily on lightweight, points-based peer bonuses and Achievers and Workhuman target larger enterprise HR buyers with deeper HRIS-embedded workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kudos is an employee recognition and rewards platform headquartered in Calgary, Alberta, Canada, generally reported as founded around 2010, that helps organizations build a culture of appreciation through structured, values-linked peer and manager recognition. The company has grown to roughly 90 employees and raised 10 million dollars in 2022 to expand its Calgary workforce and product investment.

The platform's central design choice is requiring every recognition post to be tied to one of a company's defined core values, which turns everyday praise into a structured dataset leadership can use to see which values genuinely show up in daily work, rather than functioning as a purely social thank-you wall.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Kudos combines a live peer-to-peer recognition feed with milestone and work-anniversary celebrations, manager-driven award programs, structured nomination campaigns, and configurable incentive programs aimed at reinforcing specific behaviors. An optional points-based rewards catalog lets organizations attach tangible redemptions, such as gift cards, to recognition for companies that want more than social acknowledgment.

A people analytics dashboard gives HR and leadership visibility into recognition volume, participation, and which company values are being reinforced across teams, while integrations with Microsoft Teams, Slack, and HR platforms such as ADP and UKG keep recognition embedded in tools employees already use, supported by a mobile app for distributed and frontline staff.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Kudos does not publish fixed pricing tiers; the platform uses a custom subscription model priced according to organization size, chosen features, and whether the optional rewards redemption catalog is included, typically billed monthly or annually.

One independently reported data point places real-world pricing at around three dollars per employee per month for the standard platform, though prospective buyers should expect to request a demo and custom quote rather than finding self-serve pricing on the company's website.

pros: Highly configurable to map recognition directly to company-defined core values
Rich analytics give leadership visibility into which values show up in daily work
Broad set of recognition types (peer, manager, milestone, nomination) in one platform
Integrates with major HRIS and collaboration tools like Teams, Slack, ADP, and UKG
Positioned as a data-driven engagement tool, not just a rewards catalog
cons: Pricing is not published and requires a custom quote from sales
Reward redemption catalog is an optional add-on rather than included by default
Smaller brand footprint than category leaders like Bonusly, Nectar, or Motivosity, meaning fewer public benchmarks
Effectiveness depends heavily on manager and leadership buy-in to drive adoption
Company founding and history details are less transparently documented than some larger competitors

faqs.question: What does Kudos do?
faqs.answer: Kudos is an employee recognition and rewards platform that lets coworkers and managers publicly recognize each other, tied to specific company values, while giving leadership analytics on engagement and culture.

faqs.question: How much does Kudos cost?
faqs.answer: Kudos does not publish fixed pricing; it uses a custom subscription quote based on company size and features, with one reported real-world price point of around $3 per employee per month.

faqs.question: Where is Kudos based?
faqs.answer: Kudos is headquartered in Calgary, Alberta, Canada, with additional offices supporting the US and European markets.

faqs.question: Does Kudos integrate with Microsoft Teams and Slack?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kudos integrates with collaboration tools such as Microsoft Teams and Slack as well as major HR platforms like ADP and UKG.

faqs.question: Is Kudos only for large enterprises?
faqs.answer: No, Kudos is used by organizations of varying sizes, though its analytics and configuration depth are particularly aimed at mid-size and larger companies wanting structured recognition programs.

faqs.question: Does Kudos have a rewards catalog?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kudos offers an optional points-based reward catalog that employees can redeem alongside its core social recognition feed.

use_cases.title: Remote and distributed team engagement
